item_a.name: Casper Original Mattress
item_a.image_alt: Casper Original all-foam mattress with Zoned Support system in white bedroom
pros: Zoned Support system keeps hips firmer and shoulders softer for better spinal alignment
Three-layer construction with AirScape breathable foam and memory foam for pressure relief
Excellent motion isolation — great for couples sharing a bed
Wide product lineup from budget-friendly to premium cooling hybrids
cons: Noticeably pricier than Tuft & Needle — around $400 more for the same size
Not ideal for heavier sleepers (over 230 lbs) on the all-foam Original
Less eco-friendly materials compared to Tuft & Needle
item_a.best_for: Combination sleepers and couples who want targeted zoned support and strong motion isolation in an all-foam mattress.

item_b.name: Tuft & Needle Original Mattress
item_b.image_alt: Tuft & Needle Original mattress with adaptive foam cooling technology
pros: Significantly more affordable — starts at $645 for a twin vs Casper's $995
Proprietary T&N Adaptive Foam infused with graphite and ceramic gel for superior cooling
Simple, responsive two-layer design that sleeps cooler than most all-foam beds
CertiPUR-US and Greenguard Gold certified — more eco-conscious materials
cons: No zoned support — uniform firmness may not suit all sleeping positions equally
Two-layer construction is simpler and offers less contouring than Casper's three layers
Can feel too soft for average-weight stomach sleepers
item_b.best_for: Budget-conscious shoppers and back sleepers under 230 lbs who want a cool, responsive foam mattress without breaking the bank.
shop_keywords: Tuft and Needle original mattress queen

comparison_summary: Casper edges ahead for combination sleepers and couples thanks to its Zoned Support system and three-layer construction, but Tuft & Needle punches well above its price point with superior cooling technology and eco-friendly materials. If budget is a factor, Tuft & Needle is the smarter buy — you're paying roughly $400 less for a mattress that most sleepers won't find lacking.
comparison_intro: Casper vs Tuft & Needle is one of the most searched mattress debates on the internet, and for good reason — both brands helped define the bed-in-a-box category. Whether you're trying to figure out if you should choose Casper or Tuft & Needle for your next mattress upgrade, or you just want to know which is better for your specific sleep style, the difference between Casper and Tuft & Needle comes down to more than just price. With Casper compared to Tuft & Needle on everything from foam construction to cooling performance, this breakdown will help you make a confident call.
verdict_text: Casper wins for sleepers who want zoned support and versatile comfort, but Tuft & Needle is the better value for most budget-minded buyers.

key_differences.item_a: 3 layers (AirScape polyfoam, memory foam, support base)
Yes — firmer under hips, softer under shoulders

key_differences.item_b: Graphite + ceramic gel infusion actively wicks heat
CertiPUR-US + Greenguard Gold certified; more sustainable

key_differences.item_a: Combination sleepers, couples, average-weight back/side sleepers
key_differences.item_b: Back sleepers, budget shoppers, hot sleepers under 230 lbs

seo_content:
Price is the first thing most shoppers notice, and here it's a significant gap. Tuft & Needle starts at $645 for a twin while Casper's original kicks off at $995 — a difference of $350 before you even factor in larger sizes. For a queen, you're looking at roughly $400 more for Casper. That's real money, and whether it's worth it depends entirely on what you need from a mattress.

Casper's strongest argument is its Zoned Support system. The foam is engineered to be firmer under the hips and waist while staying softer under the shoulders — a design that genuinely helps with spinal alignment, especially for back and combo sleepers. I'd give Casper the edge for anyone who frequently switches positions at night or shares the bed with a partner who does. The three-layer construction also offers more nuanced pressure relief than Tuft & Needle's simpler two-layer setup.

But Tuft & Needle vs Casper isn't just a story of more layers vs. fewer. T&N's proprietary Adaptive Foam, infused with graphite and ceramic gel, actively pulls heat away from your body — a more aggressive cooling approach than Casper's perforated foam. Hot sleepers should take note: in side-by-side testing, the Tuft & Needle consistently runs cooler.

From an environmental standpoint, Tuft & Needle holds an edge too. It carries both CertiPUR-US and Greenguard Gold certifications, signaling a more thoughtful approach to material sourcing compared to Casper's standard CertiPUR-US certification.

For heavier sleepers (over 230 lbs), neither the original Casper nor original Tuft & Needle is a perfect fit — both are best-suited for lightweight to average-weight body types. If that's your situation, both brands offer upgraded hybrid models worth exploring instead.

Looking at Casper vs Tuft & Needle through a value lens, the Tuft & Needle Original is one of the better sub-$700 foam mattresses on the market. You give up zoned support and one foam layer, but you get a cooler, eco-friendlier bed at a price that leaves room in the budget for quality sheets or a solid bed frame. For most people — especially first-time mattress buyers or anyone furnishing a guest room — that's the smarter call.

Casper is the pick for sleepers who want a more engineered feel and can justify the higher price. The Zoned Support is a real differentiator, not just marketing language.

faq.q: Is Casper better than Tuft & Needle?
faq.a: For combination sleepers and couples, Casper has the edge thanks to its three-layer construction and Zoned Support system, which keeps the hips firmer and shoulders softer for better spinal alignment. However, Tuft & Needle is better for hot sleepers and budget-conscious shoppers, offering superior active cooling and a lower price point — around $400 less for the same size.

faq.q: Should I choose Casper or Tuft & Needle?
faq.a: Choose Casper if you're a combo sleeper who wants targeted zoned support and don't mind paying a premium. Choose Tuft & Needle if you sleep hot, prefer a simpler but highly responsive foam feel, or want to spend significantly less without sacrificing quality — the T&N Original is genuinely excellent for most back and side sleepers under 230 lbs.

faq.q: What is the difference between Casper and Tuft & Needle?
faq.a: The four biggest differences are: (1) Price — Tuft & Needle costs roughly $400 less for a queen; (2) Construction — Casper has three foam layers vs. T&N's two; (3) Support — Casper uses a Zoned Support system while Tuft & Needle has uniform firmness; and (4) Cooling — Tuft & Needle uses graphite and ceramic gel for active heat-wicking, while Casper relies on perforated foam for passive airflow.

faq.q: Which mattress is better for hot sleepers — Casper or Tuft & Needle?
faq.a: Tuft & Needle is the better choice for hot sleepers. Its Adaptive Foam is infused with graphite and ceramic gel that actively draws heat away from the body, making it run cooler than Casper's AirScape perforated foam in most real-world comparisons.

faq.q: Do Casper and Tuft & Needle have the same warranty and trial period?
faq.a: Yes — both brands offer a 100-night sleep trial and a 10-year warranty on their original mattresses. Both also include free shipping to the contiguous US, though additional charges apply for Alaska and Hawaii.
